label: ai
topic: Overcoming fear of public speaking to become student body president
text:
I used to rehearse silence.

In middle school, when teachers asked for volunteers to read aloud, I would drop my eyes to the desk, tracing the wood grain as if it were a map out of the moment. My throat tightened at the mere thought of my name being called. Words that felt clear and articulate in my head would tangle into something unrecognizable the second I imagined speaking them out loud. Once, during a class presentation, I stood frozen at the front of the room, gripping the edges of a poster board so hard my fingers ached. My voice never came. The teacher gently suggested I sit down, and the relief I felt was almost as overwhelming as the shame.

For years, I built my identity around that fear. I was "the quiet one," the observer, the person who listened but never led. It felt safer that way.

Everything shifted the day a teacher insisted I run for student council. I laughed it off at first—it felt like a misunderstanding of who I was. But she didn't drop it. She told me that leadership didn't start with confidence; it started with willingness. Reluctantly, I agreed to try, convincing myself that losing would be easier than living with regret.

Campaign week arrived faster than I expected. My speech sat folded in my backpack for days, the paper softening at the creases as I opened and closed it without reading. The morning I was supposed to speak, I stood backstage listening to the muffled hum of the auditorium. My hands shook so much I had to press them against the podium to steady myself.

When I finally walked out, the lights were blinding. I could barely see the faces in the crowd, but I could feel them—hundreds of eyes waiting. For a moment, I considered turning around. Then I remembered every time I had stayed silent, every opportunity I had let pass. I unfolded my speech.

My voice trembled at first, thin and uncertain. But as I moved through the words, something unexpected happened: I stopped thinking about myself. I started thinking about what I wanted to say—about the changes I hoped to make, about the students who felt invisible the way I once had. My voice steadied. By the time I reached the end, I realized I hadn't looked down once.

I didn't just win that election—I kept running. Each speech became a little easier, each meeting a little less intimidating. By senior year, I stood at the same podium as student body president, addressing an auditorium that no longer felt like a threat. It felt like a responsibility.

The fear never fully disappeared. Even now, before I speak, there's a moment where my chest tightens and my palms grow damp. But I've learned that courage isn't the absence of fear—it's the decision to move forward despite it.

I used to rehearse silence. Now, I rehearse impact.
